page: Vultr voc-m-8c-64gb-1600s-amd is an x86_64 server instance within the Optimized Cloud Compute family. It features 8 vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC Milan processor running at 2.0 GHz with 8 physical cores and a single thread per core. The instance is configured with a shared CPU allocation. It includes 64.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Local storage consists of a bundled 1600 GB NVMe SSD, and no GPU hardware is present.

Benchmark data indicates a strong memory subsystem, with uncached large-block read and write memory bandwidth scoring in the top 25% tier. The Passmark memory score also places in the top 25%. However, CPU performance presents a distinct tradeoff. While stress-ng single-core CPU performance ranks in the top 10% tier, multi-core CPU performance and Passmark CPU scores fall into the middle 50% average tier, which is consistent with the shared CPU allocation model.

This hardware profile is structured for memory-intensive workloads that require substantial local storage but do not demand sustained, dedicated multi-core CPU performance. It is suited for database operations, caching layers such as Redis, and memory-heavy web serving. The shared CPU allocation offers a qualitative cost advantage, providing a high density of RAM and NVMe storage per resource unit compared to dedicated-core alternatives.
description: Vultr voc-m-8c-64gb-1600s-amd is an x86_64 server in the Optimized Cloud Compute family, featuring 8 vCPUs on an AMD EPYC Milan processor with a shared CPU allocation. It is equipped with 64.0 GB of RAM and a 1600 GB NVMe SSD. Benchmark results show strong memory performance, with uncached large-block read and write bandwidth in the top 25%, alongside top 10% single-core CPU performance. However, multi-core CPU performance remains in the average tier due to the shared CPU architecture. This configuration offers high memory and storage density, making it suitable for memory-intensive workloads, database operations, and caching layers that benefit from large local storage without requiring dedicated multi-core CPU resources.
